Concrete KillSwitchOperatorAlertSink email/SMS transport — atp-notification is a stub today; the email/SMS dispatcher is the deferred SRS-NOTIF-001 fan-out. The port is fallible (returns Result<(), KillSwitchSideEffectError>) and resolve_kill_switch_timeout records the outcome on KillSwitchTimeoutEvent.operator_alert; the typed transport-failure taxonomy lands with the SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher.

SRS-LOG-001 persistent sink consuming KillSwitchTimeoutEvent (the kill-switch-activation log event class, SYS-61) and SRS-UI-001 dashboard kill-switch pane (UI-4 'see cancellation, liquidation submission, timeout, notification, and disconnect status').

SRS-SAFE-002 fault-injection scenario + performance test driving resolve_kill_switch_timeout through the live kill-switch + IB-adapter runtime; ERR-8 stays passes:false until that end-to-end path exists.

backtest_contract.description: SRS-BT-001 (backtest Python strategies against stored data and user-uploaded Parquet data over configurable date ranges). This SDK-surface ships a GENUINELY RUNNABLE deterministic backtest engine in crates/atp-simulation (module `backtest`): BacktestEngine::run takes a BacktestRequest (a configurable inclusive DateRange, a BacktestDataSource = SystemData | UploadedData, and a starting cash balance), a BarSource, and a BacktestStrategy; it restricts replay to the configurable window, replays bars in deterministic timestamp order, drives the strategy bar-by-bar, applies deterministic fills, and produces a real trade log (Vec<Fill>) + equity curve (Vec<EquityPoint>) + final equity. All money math is in integer minor units (i64) with i128 intermediates and checked_add / checked_sub / try_from arithmetic (the free fn checked_notional) — never f64 — so it is exact and overflow-safe (BacktestError::Overflow). The engine fails closed on an empty symbol (EmptySymbol), an inverted window (InvalidDateRange), and a window with no bars (EmptyData). Because the BarSource (uploaded Parquet / system catalog) is a trust boundary, the engine validates provenance — BarSource::source() must equal request.data_source or the run fails closed (DataSourceMismatch), so a result can never misreport which dataset it ran against — and also rejects a source that returns a bar for a different symbol (UnexpectedSymbol — never silently trade foreign prices), duplicate bars for one symbol+timestamp (DuplicateBar — a duplicate market record would double-fill one instant and make replay order-dependent), and any replayed bar with a non-positive close price (NonPositivePrice — a negative close would otherwise flip a buy's checked_sub into a cash-fabricating addition) before any fill is applied. The strategy port BacktestStrategy::on_bar is fallible (returns Result<i64, BacktestError>): because the deferred Python host can raise / time out / fail to marshal, a strategy failure surfaces as StrategyFailed and the engine aborts before applying any fill, never silently treating a failure as a 0 delta. The engine also fails closed (TooManyBars) when the IN-WINDOW replay set exceeds MAX_BACKTEST_BARS — counted AFTER date-range filtering (bars.retain), so the cap is consistent with source-superset tolerance (a large out-of-window response never fails a valid narrow backtest) and bounds the sort + equity-curve allocation; BacktestEngine::with_max_bars sets a custom cap. Bounding the source's own materialization of the raw response needs the full streaming/bounded-cursor BarSource, deferred to the real Parquet reader (see deferred[]). The engine is co-located with the Internal Simulation Engine because SRS-BT-003 mandates the SAME transaction-cost model family for paper simulation and backtesting (home_rationale). Identical inputs produce an identical BacktestResult — the deterministic-replay seam SRS-BT-010 owns. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-001 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-001 passes:false because the launch + data + strategy-execution halves are deferred behind the BarSource / BacktestStrategy ports (see deferred[]): there is no live REST/dashboard launch surface, no real Parquet reader, and no Rust<->Python strategy host yet.
backtest_contract.home_rationale: The SRS module table lists Backtesting and Optimization as a distinct Rust runtime, but SRS-BT-003 mandates the same transaction-cost model family for the internal simulation engine and for backtesting. Co-locating the backtest engine with the InternalSimulationEngine in atp-simulation keeps that shared fill/cost model in one crate; this is the operator-directed home for the slice.

deferred.what: The concrete BarSource implementations — the system catalog reader backed by the unified historical data interface (HistoricalDataAdapter, atp-adapters) and the real arrow/parquet-backed reader for user-uploaded Parquet files (a new external Rust dependency that needs SyRS scope approval; the workspace currently has zero third-party crates) — and the concrete BacktestStrategy that marshals on_bar calls to a Python strategy running under the orchestrator (the Rust<->Python execution boundary, e.g. PyO3 or a subprocess strategy host, also SyRS-gated). The engine models the deterministic replay + integer-money fill loop only; the runtime that supplies real bars and runs real Python strategies flips SRS-BT-001 passes:true.

The live REST handler for POST /api/v1/backtests (declared today as route metadata in python/atp_api/routes.py, which explicitly does not start an HTTP server) and the dashboard backtest controls with start/end date pickers. The acceptance criterion 'start and end dates are selectable through API and dashboard' needs this operator-interface runtime; the engine already accepts a configurable DateRange and echoes it on BacktestResult.range.

BarSource::bars takes a max_bars cap (the engine passes self.max_bars) and its contract requires a source to fail closed with TooManyBars before materializing an oversized Vec; the engine re-checks the in-window count as defense in depth. The FULLY streaming/bounded sorted cursor that never materializes a Vec at all (chunked record-batch iteration) is still deferred to the real arrow/parquet-backed reader, because that shape depends on the library's reader API; the bounded max_bars parameter is the forward-compatible seam, and the Vec port has no concrete implementors yet, so it is not a baked-in external contract. Likewise the fallible strategy port surfaces failures as the single StrategyFailed { reason } today; the typed StrategyTimedOut / StrategyPanicked / marshaling-error taxonomy lands with the concrete Rust<->Python strategy host (same reason-string-now / typed-later pattern as the kill_switch_timeout_contract probe error). Raised by /codex:adversarial-review round-2: the symbol/duplicate/non-positive-price/strategy-failure guards were added in this slice; the streaming-cursor redesign is deferred per the scope decision.

SRS-BT-002 is now IMPLEMENTED on this engine: the configurable transaction-cost model family (commission / slippage / spread-impact, SyRS-default matching) lives in the `cost` module and BacktestEngine::run applies it per fill via the per-run BacktestRequest.cost_config — see the backtest_cost_contract block. BT-001's own engine tests still run frictionless (CostConfig::zero). Still deferred: the operator override SURFACE that lets an operator pick a cost model per run (SRS-BT-002, SRS-API-001/SRS-UI) and the internal simulation engine sharing the SAME family for paper fills (SRS-BT-003).

The full performance metric set (Sharpe, Sortino, alpha, beta, maximum drawdown, annualized return/volatility, win rate). The engine emits the raw trade log + equity curve those metrics are computed from; the metric computations are SRS-BT-004's concern.

Persisted, queryable backtest results (parameters, metrics, trade log, equity curve, benchmark comparison, strategy code version, timestamp). BacktestResult is the in-memory shape; the durable store + query API are SRS-BT-009.

The end-to-end deterministic-backtest guarantee under the real Python strategy host (two identical runs produce identical trade logs, equity curves, and metrics). This slice demonstrates determinism for the Rust engine (stable sort_by_key replay + integer money math); the guarantee across the full Python execution path is SRS-BT-010.

backtest_cost_contract.description: SRS-BT-002 (apply configurable commission, slippage, and spread-impact models to backtests). The configurable transaction-cost model FAMILY lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `cost`) and is APPLIED by the runnable backtest engine (module `backtest`). CostConfig bundles three configurable models: CommissionModel (default IbTiered = the published Interactive Brokers tiered schedule — IB_TIERED_RATE_CENTIMINOR_PER_SHARE per share, floored at IB_TIERED_MIN_PER_ORDER_MINOR per order, capped at IB_TIERED_MAX_PCT_BPS of trade value, SYS-15a), SlippageModel (default NotionalBps at DEFAULT_SLIPPAGE_BPS = 0.05% of trade notional, SYS-15b), and SpreadImpactModel (default ObservedOrFallbackBps = half the observed bid-ask spread per share when the bar carries one, else DEFAULT_SPREAD_FALLBACK_BPS = 0.10% of notional, SYS-15c). CostConfig::default() is the SyRS baseline encoded as named constants, so the default is PROVABLY the published value; an operator overrides any model for an individual run (SYS-15d) by building a different CostConfig on BacktestRequest.cost_config — no strategy code changes. All cost math is integer minor units (i128 intermediates, deterministic round-half-up division via div_round_half_up, i64::try_from narrowing -> CostError::Overflow; never f64); every cost is non-negative and the engine always SUBTRACTS it from cash via checked_sub, so a cost can never fabricate cash. The engine fails closed before any fill on a negative configured parameter (CostConfig::validate -> CostError::NegativeParameter) and on a corrupt negative observed bid-ask spread on ANY bar (BacktestError::NegativeSpread, mirroring the BT-001 non-positive-price guard). cost.rs is the SAME transaction-cost model family the internal simulation engine shares for paper fills (SRS-BT-003 deferred half — see deferred[]). The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-002 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-002 passes:false because the operator override SURFACE (the REST/CLI/dashboard controls that let an operator pick a cost model per run, SRS-API-001/SRS-UI) and the real data + Python strategy runtime (SRS-BT-001-runtime) are still deferred.

shared_family.note: SRS-BT-003 shares the cost::CostConfig family for internal simulation paper fills.

deferred.what: The REST/CLI/dashboard controls that let an operator select or override the commission, slippage, and spread-impact models for an individual backtest run (SYS-15d). The engine already accepts a per-run CostConfig on BacktestRequest and applies it to fills; the operator-facing surface that constructs that override from a form / CLI flag / API payload is the deferred half, so feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-002 passes:false.

IMPLEMENTED in SESSION 39 (SRS-BT-003): the internal simulation engine now consumes this exact cost::CostConfig family for paper fills via the `sim` module -- PaperSimulationEngine::simulate_fill calls the IDENTICAL CostConfig::cost_breakdown entry point the backtest engine calls, and defaults to the same SyRS baseline (SYS-15e). See `sim_cost_contract`. The remaining SRS-BT-003 deferrals (SYS-83 limit/stop/fill-probability/volume-cap fill models, live-market-data fills, the full SYS-84 ledger, paper-state persistence, and the operator per-strategy override surface) live in sim_cost_contract.deferred[].

IbTiered models the published base (<=300k shares/month) per-share rate, the per-order minimum, and the 1%-of-trade-value cap. The cumulative monthly-volume tier ladder (cheaper per-share rates at higher monthly volume) needs cross-run state and is deferred; the per-run rate/floor/cap is the SyRS default baseline.

Same deferral as SRS-BT-001: the real Parquet / system-catalog BarSource and the Rust<->Python strategy host. The cost models are applied by the runnable engine over the in-memory port fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime.

sim_cost_contract.description: SRS-BT-003 (use the same transaction-cost model family for internal simulation and backtesting unless configured otherwise; SyRS SYS-15e / SYS-83d; StRS SN-1.03 / SN-1.29). The internal simulation engine applies the SAME transaction-cost model FAMILY (crates/atp-simulation module `cost`, SRS-BT-002) the runnable backtest engine applies, via the new module `sim` paper-fill path. PaperSimulationEngine holds a `cost_config: CostConfig` that derives Default = CostConfig::default(), the IDENTICAL SyRS baseline the backtest engine defaults to (SYS-15e 'same default ... unless explicitly configured otherwise'); an operator overrides it per strategy via with_cost_config without touching strategy code. simulate_fill computes the per-fill CostBreakdown by calling self.cost_config.cost_breakdown(quantity, price_minor, observed_spread_minor) -- the IDENTICAL entry point BacktestEngine::run calls -- and folds the total into a signed cash_delta_minor that ALWAYS SUBTRACTS the cost (a buy pays notional plus cost; a sell receives proceeds less cost), so a cost can never fabricate cash. All money math is integer minor units (i128::from intermediates, i64::try_from -> SimError::Overflow, checked_*; never f64). The fill fails closed before computing any cost on an empty symbol (SimError::EmptySymbol), a non-positive price (SimError::NonPositivePrice), a negative observed spread (SimError::NegativeSpread), and a misconfigured negative cost parameter (SimError::Cost(CostError::NegativeParameter)). The minimal PaperLedger accumulates cash, position, and commission_paid (the full SYS-84 virtual ledger is SRS-SIM-003). The Rust integration test srs_bt_003_shared_cost_family asserts the paper fill's commission / slippage / spread-impact EQUAL the backtest fill's for identical config and inputs (the SRS-BT-003 acceptance criterion). The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-003 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-003 passes:false because the SYS-83 limit/stop/fill-probability/volume-cap fill models, live-market-data fills, the full SYS-84 ledger, paper-state persistence (SYS-89), and the operator per-strategy override surface (SRS-API-001/SRS-UI) plus the Python strategy runtime are still deferred.

shared_family.note: lib.rs re-exports both `pub mod cost;` and `pub mod sim;`; the internal simulation engine and the backtest engine apply the SAME cost::CostConfig family through the identical CostConfig::cost_breakdown entry point (SRS-BT-003 / SYS-15e).

deferred.what: The SYS-83 market/limit/stop/stop-limit fill models, the configurable fill-probability model, the bar-volume fill cap, and fills driven by live bid/ask/last from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70). This slice computes the shared cost decomposition over explicit (quantity, price, spread) fill inputs; the order-type fill logic, volume constraints, and live-data fill source are SRS-SIM-002's half.

The full SYS-84 virtual position ledger -- per-symbol average cost, realized and unrealized P&L marked to live data, independent per strategy. PaperLedger here is a minimal cash + position + commission-paid accumulator that proves the shared family's commissions actually accumulate; the full ledger is SRS-SIM-003.
Persisting and restoring the paper ledger, pending simulated orders, and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89).

The REST/CLI/dashboard controls that let an operator select or override the per-strategy cost model. The engine already accepts a per-strategy CostConfig via PaperSimulationEngine::with_cost_config; the operator-facing surface that constructs that override is the deferred half.

Same deferral as SRS-BT-001/002: the Python strategy host that drives paper fills end to end. The shared cost family is exercised over in-memory fill fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime, so feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-003 passes:false.

sim_order_contract.description: SRS-SIM-001 (simulate paper strategy orders locally without routing to any brokerage; SyRS SYS-82 local paper order execution, SYS-3 order types, SYS-4 multi-leg composite; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-1.08 / SN-1.24). The internal simulation engine's paper order-intake path lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `paper_order`) and adds an `accept_order` method to PaperSimulationEngine that accepts a PaperOrderRequest (a single OrderLeg or a MultiLeg composite), validates every leg, and returns an OrderRouting. The CRITICAL safety design: OrderRouting has exactly ONE variant, InternalSimulation, so there is structurally no Broker/Ib variant to construct and a paper order can never reach a brokerage -- 'creates no IB API order calls' (SRS-SIM-001) is a COMPILE-TIME guarantee, not a runtime check. The atp-simulation crate also has no dependency on atp-execution or atp-adapters (the live/broker path), reinforcing the no-broker-route invariant at the crate boundary. accept_order processes every OrderType (Market, Limit, Stop, StopLimit) and AssetClass (Equity, Option); a MultiLeg request routes as one composite transaction (composite: true, SYS-4) so its legs fill atomically, and because SYS-4 / SRS-EXE-004 scope multi-leg to OPTIONS composites a MultiLeg must carry two or more legs that are all options. Intake fails closed before routing on an empty symbol (OrderError::EmptySymbol), a non-positive quantity (NonPositiveQuantity), a non-positive limit/stop price (NonPositiveLimitPrice / NonPositiveStopPrice), an empty multi-leg request (EmptyMultiLeg), a single-leg composite (SingleLegComposite), and a non-option composite leg (NonOptionCompositeLeg); the per-leg guards live in the private validate_leg helper and the composite-shape guards in accept_order. Every price an order carries is an integer minor unit with the _minor suffix (limit_price_minor, stop_price_minor); intake performs no fill arithmetic and the module contains no f64. The Rust integration test srs_sim_001_paper_order_intake asserts every order type / asset class / single + multi-leg request routes to InternalSimulation and that the routing enum exposes no broker variant. The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-001 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-001 passes:false because the SYS-83 fill TRIGGERING (price-cross fills, fill probability, bar-volume cap) and live-market-data fills are SRS-SIM-002, the full SYS-84 virtual ledger is SRS-SIM-003, paper-state persistence (SYS-89) is SRS-SIM-004, the orchestrator routing of all non-live strategies into this engine is SRS-EXE-002, and the Python strategy runtime that submits these orders end to end is still deferred.

deferred.what: The SYS-83 fill TRIGGERING for market/limit/stop/stop-limit orders (fill at ask/bid for market, fill on price cross for limit, trigger on stop cross), the configurable fill-probability model, the bar-volume fill cap, and fills driven by live bid/ask/last from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70). This slice accepts, validates, and routes the orders; the logic that turns a routed order into a fill is SRS-SIM-002's half.

Applying accepted/filled orders to the full SYS-84 virtual position ledger (per-symbol average cost, realized and unrealized P&L), independent per strategy. SRS-SIM-001 stops at routing; the ledger application is SRS-SIM-003.
Persisting and restoring pending simulated orders and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89).

The Strategy Orchestrator wiring that routes ALL non-live strategy orders into this engine. accept_order is the per-engine intake; the system-level guarantee that every non-live strategy lands here (and only the single live strategy reaches IB) is SRS-EXE-002 / SRS-EXE-001.

Same deferral as SRS-BT/SRS-SIM siblings: the Python strategy host that actually submits these orders end to end. Intake is exercised over in-memory request fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime, so feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-001 passes:false.

sim_fill_contract.description: SRS-SIM-002 (simulate fills using live market data and configurable fill models; SyRS SYS-83 fill simulation, SYS-87 realism constraints; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-1.03). The internal simulation engine's fill-model / triggering path lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `fill_model`) and adds an `evaluate_fill` method to PaperSimulationEngine that turns a routed OrderType (from the SRS-SIM-001 intake path) plus a MarketSnapshot (bid, ask, last, volume from the SYS-70 subscription manager) into a FillDecision. The SYS-83 fill rules: a Market order fills at the ask (buy) or bid (sell); a Limit order fills only when the market crosses the limit under the configurable per-strategy LimitFillModel and takes the limit price as its conservative no-improvement reference. Two behavior-changing deterministic limit models ship: ImmediateOnCross (the SYS-83b default) fills a touch at the limit (buy when ask <= limit, sell when bid >= limit), while RequireThroughCross fills only on a strict cross (buy when ask < limit, sell when bid > limit), so the two configs produce different decisions on the same touch snapshot. a Stop order triggers when the last trade crosses the stop (buy when last >= stop, sell when last <= stop) and then fills as a market order; a StopLimit order triggers at the stop then rests as a limit. The SYS-87b volume constraint is enforced both per-order and in aggregate: evaluate_fill caps a single order at the bar volume (fill_quantity = min(requested, bar_volume)), while evaluate_fill_against_budget caps each fill at a per-bar BarVolumeBudget's REMAINING volume and CONSUMES it, so the SUM of fills against one bar (the SYS-87b 'bar period') cannot exceed its observed volume even across orders; evaluate_fill is exactly evaluate_fill_against_budget with a fresh per-call budget. The budget is BOUND to its bar: evaluate_fill_against_budget fails closed with FillModelError::BudgetSnapshotMismatch when the budget's observed_bar_volume does not equal the snapshot's bar_volume, so a stale or oversized budget cannot be used against a thinner bar to fill past its observed volume. An exhausted bar yields NoFillReason::ZeroVolume, the trigger/cross test runs BEFORE the cap so an order that never crosses reports LimitNotCrossed / StopNotTriggered and consumes no budget, and the bar-replay loop that sequences orders within a bar is the deferred SRS-SIM-003 owner. evaluate_fill is &self with no clock or randomness, so it is deterministic. It fails closed BEFORE any fill decision on a non-positive requested quantity (FillModelError::NonPositiveQuantity), a non-positive limit/stop price on the raw order type (NonPositiveLimitPrice / NonPositiveStopPrice via validate_order_type, so a negative limit can never cross a valid snapshot and return a fill at that negative price), a non-positive quote (NonPositiveQuote), a crossed book (CrossedBook), and a negative bar volume (NegativeVolume); the order-price guards live in validate_order_type and the snapshot guards in validate_snapshot. A FillDecision::Filled feeds simulate_fill (SRS-BT-003), so a triggered fill flows through the SAME transaction-cost family the backtest engine applies and stays entirely inside the internal simulation engine -- it creates no IB API order call, reusing the SRS-SIM-001 no-broker-route crate-boundary guarantee (atp-simulation depends on neither atp-execution nor atp-adapters). Every price is an integer minor unit with the _minor suffix; the module contains no f64. The Rust integration test srs_sim_002_fill_models asserts every order type resolves a deterministic decision, limit/stop orders hold until the market crosses, the two limit fill models produce different decisions on a touch, the per-order volume cap is enforced, the aggregate of fills against one BarVolumeBudget cannot exceed the bar volume, a budget that does not match the snapshot's bar volume fails closed (cannot overfill a thinner bar), a filled decision flows through the cost family, corrupt market data fails closed, and no malformed (negative/zero) limit/stop price can produce a fill. The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-002 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-002 passes:false because the SYS-87a market-hours gating needs the SYS-50 trading calendar, the SYS-87c stale-data threshold rejection needs the SYS-39 freshness wiring, the live bid/ask/last feed comes from the SYS-70 subscription manager (the snapshot is an in-memory fixture today), the STOCHASTIC fill-probability model (SYS-83b) needs a seeded RNG and stays deferred (the two deterministic LimitFillModel variants that ship already satisfy the configurable-fill-model requirement), the full SYS-84 virtual ledger is SRS-SIM-003, paper-state persistence (SYS-89) is SRS-SIM-004, the orchestrator routing of all non-live strategies is SRS-EXE-002, and the Python strategy runtime is still deferred.

deferred.what: Gating simulated fills to market hours per the trading calendar (SYS-87a). evaluate_fill models the price/volume realism of a fill; the market-hours gate needs the SYS-50 trading-calendar service, which is unbuilt, so it is deferred to SRS-MD / the calendar feature.

Rejecting order submissions when market data is stale per the configurable staleness threshold (SYS-87c / SYS-39a). This slice validates the SHAPE of the snapshot (positive quotes, an uncrossed book, non-negative volume) but does not yet consult a freshness clock; the staleness-threshold check belongs on the SYS-70 fan-out / freshness_contract seam and is deferred.

Sourcing the MarketSnapshot's live bid/ask/last/volume from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70) fan-out. The snapshot is an in-memory fixture today; the live feed wiring is the SYS-70 / SRS-MD owner's half.

The STOCHASTIC fill-probability variant of LimitFillModel (SYS-83b 'subject to configurable fill probability model'). Two DETERMINISTIC, behavior-changing limit models already ship (ImmediateOnCross fills a touch, RequireThroughCross requires a strict cross), so the SRS-SIM-002 configurable-fill-model acceptance path is met; a probabilistic model that fills a crossed limit with probability p < 1 needs a seeded RNG and is deferred to keep evaluate_fill deterministic. The LimitFillModel enum is the structural seam it will extend.

Applying filled decisions to the full SYS-84 virtual position ledger (per-symbol average cost, realized and unrealized P&L), independent per strategy. evaluate_fill stops at the fill decision; simulate_fill records cash/position/commission into the minimal PaperLedger seam, and the full ledger is SRS-SIM-003.
Persisting and restoring pending simulated orders and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89).

The Strategy Orchestrator wiring that routes ALL non-live strategy orders through this fill model. evaluate_fill is the per-engine fill surface; the system-level guarantee that every non-live strategy fills here (and only the single live strategy reaches IB) is SRS-EXE-002 / SRS-EXE-001.

Same deferral as the SRS-BT / SRS-SIM siblings: the Python strategy host that submits the orders these fills satisfy end to end. The fill model is exercised over in-memory snapshot fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime, so feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-002 passes:false.

virtual_ledger_contract.description: SRS-SIM-003 (maintain an independent virtual position ledger for each paper strategy; SyRS SYS-84; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-1.07). The internal simulation engine's per-strategy virtual position ledger lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `virtual_ledger`) and consumes a priced sim::PaperFill (the SRS-BT-003 cost path) without any new dependency. Three nested layers, each isolated from the next: VirtualPosition is one symbol's signed quantity (i64), signed cost_basis_minor (i128, the source of truth for average cost: positive for a long = cash laid out, negative for a short = proceeds received), accumulated realized_pnl_minor (i128, GROSS of every transaction cost), and the FULL transaction-cost decomposition the cost family charges -- commission_paid_minor, slippage_paid_minor, and spread_impact_paid_minor (each i128), summed by transaction_cost_paid_minor. Tracking every cost component (not just commission) keeps the ledger faithful to the simulator: a closed position's realized_pnl_minor minus transaction_cost_paid_minor reconciles EXACTLY with the sum of the fills' cash_delta_minor (the simulator's actual cash impact), so no charged cost (commission, slippage, or spread) silently disappears from the ledger. StrategyLedger holds one paper strategy's VirtualPosition values keyed by symbol (positions: HashMap<String, VirtualPosition>); the symbol is canonicalized via canonical_symbol (symbol.trim().to_uppercase(), the same policy as atp_types::SecurityKey) before every key and lookup, so AAPL / aapl / ' AAPL ' resolve to ONE position instead of splitting one security's quantity and P&L across aliases. The ledger's instrument identity is the canonical symbol STRING, which uniquely identifies the instrument under the system's symbol conventions (equities by ticker, options by the full OCC contract string such as 'AAPL  240119C00190000', so an option and its underlying do NOT share a key). A SecurityKey is not used directly because the simulated fill (sim::PaperFill) carries a bare symbol with NO asset class and SecurityKey rejects options that SRS-SIM-001 intake accepts; formal (symbol + asset_class + full option-contract) keying needs the upstream PaperFill / simulate_fill cost-path contract (SRS-BT-003 / SRS-SIM-001) to carry that identity, which is the Data Layer's normalization responsibility and is DEFERRED (see deferred[]). VirtualLedgerBook holds every paper strategy's ledger keyed by StrategyId (ledgers: HashMap<StrategyId, StrategyLedger>), and apply_fill routes a fill to exactly one strategy's ledger via self.ledgers.get_mut(strategy) (existing) or self.ledgers.insert(strategy.clone(), ledger) on first touch, so one strategy's fills can never read or mutate another's quantities or P&L, and the book holds NO IB / broker account position at all (it imports no broker, adapter, or live-position type), so a virtual position is structurally independent of the IB account (SYS-84). The book and the per-strategy ledger both insert a new entry ONLY after the fill applies successfully (the fresh value is built and ledger.apply_fill(fill)? / position.apply_fill(fill)? propagates the error BEFORE the insert), so a rejected first fill never leaves a phantom strategy or phantom symbol behind to pollute later metrics, persistence, or orchestrator accounting. Average-cost accounting on apply_fill: opening or adding in the same direction grows cost_basis by q*px and quantity by q with no realized P&L; reducing or closing (q opposes the held quantity, |q| <= |held|) releases the PROPORTIONAL slice of the basis cost_removed = cost_basis * |q| / |held| (cost_basis.checked_mul(fill_abs) / held_abs), recognises realized += -(q*px) - cost_removed, drops cost_removed from the basis, and adds q to the quantity, so closing the whole position releases EXACTLY the whole basis and leaves cost_basis at 0; flipping through zero (|q| > |held|) fully closes the existing position (releasing all its basis and recognising its realized P&L) then opens the remainder q_open = q + held at px as a fresh position. Realized P&L is kept GROSS of commission and commission_paid_minor accumulates SEPARATELY (SYS-84 lists them as distinct fields). average_cost_minor returns cost_basis / quantity (truncated, None when flat) -- a DERIVED view of the exact signed cost basis. The rounding policy is chosen for EXACT BASIS CONSERVATION: cost_removed truncates toward zero and the remainder stays in the residual basis, so across any fill sequence realized P&L plus the remaining basis equals the exact cash from prices and a full close always lands at cost_basis == 0 (no value created or destroyed). The deliberate trade-off is that the truncated per-unit average_cost_minor can shift by up to one minor unit on a partial close even with no acquisition; the basis itself is always exact, and the conservation invariant is pinned by a property-style unit test over many generated fill sequences (including an indivisible basis-32-over-3 case). unrealized_pnl_minor marks the open position to market against the live MarketSnapshot's last trade price (mark * quantity - cost_basis, exact and correct for both longs and shorts, 0 when flat). StrategyLedger and VirtualLedgerBook expose a SYMBOL-KEYED unrealized_pnl_minor(symbol, snapshot) surface that selects the position BY the named symbol, so a quote is never applied to a different instrument's position by mistake; rejecting a wrong-symbol quote by its CONTENT needs the SRS-SIM-002 MarketSnapshot to carry instrument identity (it is an identity-free fixture today), which is deferred with the SYS-70 live feed. It fails closed BEFORE any mutation on a non-positive fill price (LedgerError::NonPositiveFillPrice), a zero-quantity fill (LedgerError::ZeroQuantityFill), a negative transaction-cost component (LedgerError::NegativeCost{component} -- PaperFill fields are public, so the ledger validates commission, slippage, and spread_impact rather than trusting them, since a negative value would decrease its accumulator and could fabricate cash on reconciliation), a cash_delta_minor that disagrees with -(notional) - total_cost (LedgerError::InconsistentCashDelta -- the ledger does not accumulate cash from the public cash_delta field but enforces the simulate_fill invariant on it before mutating, so a tampered/inconsistent fill cannot silently break the reconciliation guarantee), an empty symbol (LedgerError::EmptySymbol, at the StrategyLedger seam), a non-positive mark (LedgerError::NonPositiveMark), and money-math overflow (LedgerError::Overflow). VirtualPosition::apply_fill is ATOMIC: it computes every prospective value (quantity, cost basis, realized P&L, commission) into locals with checked arithmetic and commits them to the position only after EVERY checked op succeeds, so an overflow in the basis/quantity accounting can never leave a moved commission behind that a retry would double-count. apply_fill is &mut self with no clock or randomness, so the ledger is deterministic. Every quantity and money figure is an integer minor unit; intermediates are i128 with checked_* accumulation; the module contains no f64. The Rust integration test srs_sim_003_virtual_ledger drives the ledger end to end from real simulate_fill output and asserts a long round trip realizes price-based P&L while commission accumulates separately, average cost blends across adds, mark-to-market values longs and shorts, a flip through zero realizes only the closed portion and reopens at the fill price, two strategies holding the same symbol stay independent (mutating one leaves the other untouched), corrupt input fails closed, aliased symbols (AAPL / aapl / ' AAPL ') share ONE position, and a rejected first fill for a brand-new strategy leaves no phantom strategy behind (strategy_count stays 0). The unit tests additionally prove a negative cost component (commission / slippage / spread) fails closed, an inconsistent cash_delta_minor fails closed, every cost component accumulates, a rejected fill rolls back atomically including the commission (a rejected fill leaves the position byte-for-byte unchanged), basis conservation holds over many generated fill sequences (full close to cost_basis == 0 with exact realized P&L, even for an indivisible basis), the ledger reconciles with the simulator -- a round trip's realized_pnl_minor minus transaction_cost_paid_minor equals the sum of the fills' cash_delta_minor, and a same-price round trip nets a loss exactly equal to the costs charged (no cost silently disappears), and the symbol-keyed marking surface selects the position named by the symbol. The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-003 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-003 passes:false because the live bid/ask/last feed that drives the mark comes from the SYS-70 subscription manager (the MarketSnapshot is an in-memory fixture today), corporate-action adjustment of virtual positions (SYS-88) is SRS-DATA-021, ledger persistence and restore (SYS-89) is SRS-SIM-004, the accumulated paper performance metrics (SYS-85) are their own feature, the orchestrator routing of all non-live strategies into this engine is SRS-EXE-002, and the Python strategy runtime is still deferred.

deferred.feature: Asset-class / option-contract instrument identity in the position key
deferred.what: The SyRS Position (Virtual) model lists asset class alongside symbol, but the ledger keys positions by the canonical symbol STRING only, because the upstream sim::PaperFill (the SRS-BT-003 cost-path output) carries a bare symbol with NO asset class. Under the system's symbol conventions distinct instruments already get distinct keys (equities by ticker, options by the full OCC contract string), so no actual merge occurs today; formal (symbol + asset_class + full option-contract) keying needs PaperFill / simulate_fill to carry that identity, which is a cross-feature change to the SRS-BT-003 / SRS-SIM-001 contract and is deferred to that owner rather than threaded through this ledger slice.

The mark-to-market surface selects the position BY symbol (StrategyLedger / VirtualLedgerBook unrealized_pnl_minor(symbol, snapshot)), so a quote is never applied to a different instrument's position. But the SRS-SIM-002 MarketSnapshot carries no instrument identity (it is an identity-free in-memory fixture pending the SYS-70 live feed), so the ledger cannot reject a snapshot whose CONTENT is actually another instrument's quote. Binding the snapshot to an instrument (so a wrong-symbol quote fails closed) needs MarketSnapshot to carry identity, which is the SRS-SIM-002 / SYS-70 owner's shape and is deferred with the live feed.

apply_fill is a PURE state transition: it applies exactly the fill it is given, so replaying an already-applied fill double-counts quantity, basis, P&L, and costs. Deduplicating duplicate delivery needs (a) a unique execution/fill identifier on sim::PaperFill -- which it does not carry -- a cross-feature change to the SRS-BT-003 cost-path contract, and (b) exactly-once delivery semantics owned by the orchestrator routing layer (SRS-EXE-002), which routes non-live strategy fills into this engine and is itself deferred. SYS-84 / SRS-SIM-003 specify the ledger's tracked fields, not delivery semantics, so dedup is deferred to the PaperFill-identity + SRS-EXE-002 owners rather than guessed at here (a content-hash dedup would wrongly drop two legitimately-identical fills).

Sourcing the MarketSnapshot's live last trade price (the mark-to-market reference) from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70) fan-out. The snapshot is an in-memory fixture today; the live feed wiring is the SYS-70 / SRS-MD owner's half, so unrealized P&L is marked against a fixture, not a live feed.

Adjusting virtual position quantities and average cost for splits, dividends, and mergers, and cancelling virtual orders for delisted securities, using the same corporate-action data as live trading and backtesting (SYS-88). This slice keeps the raw fill-driven ledger; the corporate-action adjustment is SRS-DATA-021.
Persisting and restoring the virtual ledger, pending simulated orders, and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89). The ledger is in-memory here; persistence is SRS-SIM-004.

Computing the accumulated paper performance metrics (returns, drawdown, ratios) over the ledger's realized/unrealized P&L (SYS-85). The ledger exposes the per-position P&L primitives; the metric aggregation is its own feature (the SRS-BT-004 metric family for paper).

The Strategy Orchestrator wiring that routes ALL non-live strategy fills into this ledger. VirtualLedgerBook is the per-engine ledger surface keyed by StrategyId; the system-level guarantee that every non-live strategy's fills land here (and only the single live strategy reaches IB) is SRS-EXE-002 / SRS-EXE-001.

Same deferral as the SRS-BT / SRS-SIM siblings: the Python strategy host whose orders produce the fills this ledger records end to end. The ledger is exercised over in-memory PaperFill fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime, so feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-003 passes:false.

sim_persistence_contract.description: SRS-SIM-004 (persist paper strategy simulation state; SyRS SYS-89; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-2.05). The internal simulation engine's paper-state persistence path lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `paper_state`) and consumes the SRS-SIM-003 VirtualLedgerBook without any new dependency (no serde): money stays integer minor units (i64 quantity, i128 money) with no f64. A PaperStateSnapshot is a versioned envelope holding schema_version (i64), the PersistenceConfig in force, and a clone of the VirtualLedgerBook. SYS-89 names four sub-states (virtual position ledger, pending simulated orders, accumulated metrics, and the user-state dictionary), but only the virtual ledger has a runtime type today, so the snapshot captures the FULL ledger (every strategy, every symbol, all six VirtualPosition fields) and reserves three forward-compatible, always-empty slots for the other three sub-states; deserialize fails closed (PersistenceError::UnsupportedSection) on a non-zero reserved-slot count rather than silently dropping data a v1 reader has no type to restore. PersistenceConfig carries the SYS-89 cadence: interval_secs (u64, default DEFAULT_INTERVAL_SECS = 60), restore_deadline_secs (u64, default DEFAULT_RESTORE_DEADLINE_SECS = 30), and persist_on_shutdown (bool). PersistenceConfig::new takes only the interval and the restore deadline (NOT a shutdown flag) and fails closed on a cadence that cannot meet SYS-89: a zero-second interval or restore deadline (PersistenceError::NonPositiveConfig), and a restore deadline ABOVE the 30s ceiling (PersistenceError::RestoreDeadlineTooLong), so the config CANNOT encode an SLA slower than the 30 seconds the requirement mandates (an operator may only tighten it). The persist interval has no upper ceiling because SYS-89 makes 60s the default, not a maximum, so a longer cadence is a permitted operator trade-off. Shutdown persistence is MANDATORY: SYS-89 requires state to be persisted at the interval AND on container shutdown, so persist_on_shutdown is always true and the caller cannot disable it; a snapshot that encodes shutdown persistence as disabled is out of contract and rejected on restore (PersistenceError::ShutdownPersistenceRequired). The serializer is DETERMINISTIC: HashMap iteration order is unspecified, so serialize sorts strategies by id (StrategyId is not Ord, so by as_str()) and positions by canonical symbol before emitting, so the same state always serializes to byte-identical output (an unchanged 60s checkpoint never churns). The codec is hand-rolled and dependency-free: integers are decimal lines and variable-length strings are length-prefixed (value.len().to_string()), so a canonical symbol containing spaces (an OCC option contract such as 'AAPL  240119C00190000') round-trips losslessly. The serialized frame is the MAGIC line, a 64-bit FNV-1a integrity checksum over the body, then the body; deserialize verifies the checksum BEFORE building any state, so a structurally-valid byte change (a tampered-but-sign-consistent quantity/basis pair, a flipped digit, truncation, or appended bytes) fails closed with PersistenceError::ChecksumMismatch under fault injection rather than restoring fabricated positions or P&L. The checksum is a non-cryptographic integrity guard for ACCIDENTAL corruption (the SYS-89 fault-injection criterion), not a security MAC -- defending against a deliberate tamperer who can recompute the checksum needs a keyed MAC and key management, which is out of scope for the single-user, local-only release baseline (no multi-user auth per the system constraints). capture() builds a snapshot at SCHEMA_VERSION; serialize() emits the text; deserialize() parses it back; restore() is the convenience round-trip restore(serialized) = PaperStateSnapshot::deserialize(serialized).map(PaperStateSnapshot::into_book), so restore(serialize(capture(book))) == book exactly (quantity, signed cost basis, realized P&L, and every cost component survive, including a FLAT fully-closed position that still carries realized P&L and commission). deserialize is fail-closed and atomic: it validates the magic header (MAGIC = 'ATP-PAPER-STATE') and the schema version (a foreign blob or unknown version is rejected, not mis-read: PersistenceError::CorruptSnapshot / UnknownSchemaVersion), validates the config, validates every position field invariant BEFORE the position is built (cost components non-negative; the quantity/basis biconditional (quantity == 0) iff (cost_basis_minor == 0); sign agreement between a non-flat quantity and its basis; a canonical, non-empty symbol; a non-empty strategy id; no duplicate strategy id or duplicate symbol within a strategy: PersistenceError::InconsistentField / DuplicateRecord), and requires the cursor to be exhausted at the end (trailing bytes are corrupt). It builds the whole VirtualLedgerBook in a local and returns it only on full success, so a corrupt, truncated, or tampered snapshot returns an Err and yields NO partially-restored book (never a phantom strategy or symbol). paper_state adds no broker/adapter/live-position dependency and carries no vendor SDK token, so persisted paper state is structurally independent of the IB account (the SYS-84 isolation property holds through persistence too). The Rust integration test srs_sim_004_paper_state drives capture/serialize/restore end to end from real simulate_fill output. The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-004 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-004 passes:false because the live 60s persistence timer firing inside a running container and a real restart restoring within 30s need the SRS-EXE-002 orchestrator wiring and the SYS-89 container lifecycle, the pending_orders slot needs a paper-order pending store (SRS-SIM-001 / SRS-SIM-002 own no such store yet), the metrics slot needs the SYS-85 / SRS-BT-004 paper metric family, and the user_state slot needs the Python strategy runtime.

deferred.feature: Live 60s persistence timer and 30s-restore on a real container restart
deferred.what: SYS-89 requires the state to be persisted every 60 seconds (and on shutdown) and restored within 30 seconds of a container restart. This slice ships the deterministic capture/serialize/restore codec and the cadence config, but the live timer firing inside a running container and the restart that reads the blob back need the SRS-EXE-002 orchestrator wiring and the SYS-89 container lifecycle, which own the strategy container and its restart, so the timing guarantees are deferred to that owner and feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-004 passes:false.

SYS-89 lists pending simulated orders among the persisted sub-states, but the SRS-SIM-001 / SRS-SIM-002 paper-order path only accepts and routes orders; nothing holds an accepted-but-unfilled order as runtime state, so there is no pending-order store to persist. The snapshot reserves a forward-compatible pending_orders slot (always empty in v1, fail-closed on non-empty); populating it needs a paper-order pending store owned by the SRS-SIM-001 / SRS-SIM-002 fill path.

SYS-89 lists accumulated performance metrics among the persisted sub-states, but no PaperMetrics type exists yet (the SYS-85 / SRS-BT-004 metric family computes returns, drawdown, and ratios over the ledger's P&L primitives and is its own feature). The snapshot reserves a forward-compatible metrics slot (always empty in v1, fail-closed on non-empty); populating it needs the SYS-85 / SRS-BT-004 metric family.

SYS-89 lists the user-state dictionary (the JSON-serializable key-value state a user strategy writes via the strategy API) among the persisted sub-states, but that dictionary is produced by the Python strategy runtime, which is not built yet. The snapshot reserves a forward-compatible user_state slot (always empty in v1, fail-closed on non-empty); populating it needs the SRS-SDK Python strategy host.

deferred.feature: Corporate-action adjustment of persisted positions (SYS-88 / SRS-DATA-021)
deferred.what: Adjusting the persisted virtual positions for splits, dividends, and mergers, and cancelling persisted virtual orders for delisted securities, is SYS-88 / SRS-DATA-021. This slice persists and restores the raw ledger; corporate-action adjustment is that owner's responsibility.

sim_metrics_contract.description: SRS-BT-004 (compute required backtest and paper/live performance metrics; SyRS SYS-16, SYS-86; StRS SN-1.04 / SN-1.05 / SN-1.29). The shared performance-metric family lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `metrics`) and computes the eight SYS-16 metrics -- Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, alpha, beta, maximum drawdown, annualized return, annualized volatility, and win rate -- for one completed run. SYS-86 requires the internal simulation engine to compute the SAME family for paper strategies that the backtest engine and the live dashboard compute, so the module consumes the primitives the engine already produces: a mark-to-market backtest::EquityPoint curve and a backtest::Fill trade log, both in integer minor units. The headline design decision is the numeric boundary: money enters in integer minor units (EquityPoint.equity_minor: i64, BenchmarkPoint.level_minor: i64, every Fill money field i64), but the metrics themselves are dimensionless f64 ratios -- so unlike paper_state this module DOES contain f64, which is the metric domain, not a money-correctness leak. The f64 work is made deterministic (the SRS-BT-010 criterion this family must honor): every reduction is a fixed left-to-right fold over the timestamp-ordered series, with no parallelism, no platform RNG, and no wall-clock read, so identical inputs always produce bit-identical metrics; the deserializer rejects a non-strictly-increasing timestamp (MetricsError::NonMonotonicTimestamps) so the period returns are never order-dependent. A metric that is mathematically undefined on the given input -- Sharpe/Sortino/volatility when there are fewer than two returns or zero dispersion, alpha/beta when no benchmark series was supplied or the benchmark has zero variance, win rate when no trade closed -- is reported as None, never as a fabricated 0.0 and never as a leaked NaN/inf; every computed metric is verified finite before it is returned (MetricsError::NonFiniteComputation), so a pathological input fails closed rather than emitting a poison value. Period returns require a strictly positive equity curve (MetricsError::NonPositiveEquity) because a return divides by the prior equity. Alpha and beta are defined only against a benchmark (SYS-16 computes them relative to the SYS-17 benchmark): Benchmark defaults to SPY (DEFAULT_BENCHMARK_SYMBOL) when the operator selects none and the chosen symbol is echoed on PerformanceMetrics.benchmark_symbol so a report identifies its benchmark; the caller-supplied benchmark level series must align 1:1 by timestamp with the equity curve (MetricsError::BenchmarkLengthMismatch / BenchmarkTimestampMismatch) and be strictly positive (MetricsError::NonPositiveBenchmarkLevel). The win rate is the fraction of COMPLETE round trips whose NET P&L was strictly positive. A trade is a round trip -- the flat-to-flat span over which a symbol's position leaves zero, through any number of fills, and returns to zero -- not an individual fill, so the win rate is INVARIANT to fill fragmentation: a position closed in one fill versus three volume-capped partial fills (SRS-SIM-002) is the SAME one winning/losing round trip, keeping backtest (aggregated) and paper/live (fragmented) win rates comparable (SYS-86). A round trip's net P&L is its realized cash flow minus all its transaction costs (net = -sum(signed_qty_i * price_i) - sum(cost_i) over the round trip's fills, via closing_cash_flow); because both are sums over the whole flat-to-flat span, the result is INVARIANT to the order of fills within the round trip, so it cannot silently depend on intra-bar fill ordering, and a win counts only when that NET P&L is strictly positive (a gross profit overwhelmed by costs is correctly a loss). A single fill that REVERSES through zero settles the closed round trip and opens the next; because there is no fragmentation-invariant way to split one fill's integer cost across the two round trips it spans, the reversing fill's FULL cost is deterministically attributed to the closing round trip (equivalent to writing the reversal as a close-to-flat fill with the cost on the close, then a zero-cost open), which avoids the integer-floor bias that would charge a small reversing close zero cost and record a spurious win. Symbols are canonicalized with the ledger's canonical_symbol policy (trim + upper-case) so an open on AAPL and a close on aapl close the SAME round trip (SYS-86 parity); a negative cost component is rejected (MetricsError::NegativeFillCost), a non-positive fill price is rejected (MetricsError::NonPositiveFillPrice), and the i128 money math is checked (MetricsError::Overflow). The trade log must be time-ordered: a backwards timestamp is rejected (MetricsError::NonMonotonicTradeLog), but MULTIPLE fills may share a timestamp (several orders, or volume-capped partial fills, can fill against one bar) and are applied in TRADE-LOG (slice) ORDER -- the producer's authoritative execution sequence -- so a legitimate multi-fill bar is processed in order rather than rejected, and the result is deterministic for a given trade log. The trade log and the equity curve must describe the SAME run: every fill timestamp must fall within the equity curve's observed window [first mark ts, last mark ts] or it is rejected as incoherent (MetricsError::TradeLogOutsideRun), catching a stale or mismatched (e.g. asynchronously-snapshotted later) trade log paired with a different run's equity curve so the win rate and the equity-derived metrics cannot silently disagree. The FULL atomic run-snapshot identity (a run id binding the equity curve, trade log, and metrics into one consistent snapshot) is the deferred paper/live accumulator's responsibility; this window check is the in-scope coherence guard. The pre-trade baseline is a REQUIRED compute input (starting_equity_minor: i64), NOT an implicit first curve point, so the first period's P&L (including opening-fill entry costs) can never be silently omitted: period returns are [(equity_curve[0] - starting) / starting, ...], the drawdown peak starts at the baseline (let mut peak = starting_equity_minor as f64), and annualized return runs from the baseline (let first = starting_equity_minor as f64). Producers pass their starting cash here (the backtest engine passes BacktestResult's request.starting_cash_minor), so the metric never guesses a baseline. The baseline and every NON-final mark must be strictly positive (each is the denominator of a return), but the FINAL mark may be exactly zero -- a total loss / bankruptcy is a defined -100% final return (and a 100% drawdown), so it is computed, not rejected; only a non-final non-positive mark or a negative final mark is rejected (MetricsError::NonPositiveEquity), so one bad mark never aborts an otherwise-valid run. The benchmark's pre-trade baseline is the FIRST element of benchmark_levels (its remaining points align 1:1 by timestamp with equity_curve, so benchmark_levels has equity_curve.len() + 1 entries and yields the same number of returns as the strategy). Beta is cov(strategy, benchmark) / var(benchmark); Jensen's alpha is computed on EXCESS returns over the per-period risk-free rate ((mean_strategy - rf) - beta * (mean_benchmark - rf), annualized arithmetically), since omitting the risk-free rate is only correct when rf == 0 or beta == 1. Sharpe, Sortino, and beta divide by a dispersion; rather than an exact == 0.0 denominator check (which floating-point aggregation of near-identical returns can slip past, dividing by ~1e-17 to produce an enormous but finite, meaningless ratio), a scale-aware tolerance (negligible_dispersion / DISPERSION_EPSILON, relative to the largest absolute observation) treats a noise-level standard deviation, downside deviation, or benchmark variance as zero so a near-degenerate series yields None rather than a spurious ranking-corrupting value. MetricsConfig carries the annualization factor (periods_per_year: u32, default DEFAULT_PERIODS_PER_YEAR = 252) and the per-period risk-free rate (risk_free_rate_per_period: f64, default 0.0); MetricsConfig::new fails closed on a zero annualization factor (MetricsError::NonPositivePeriodsPerYear) and a non-finite or <= -100%/period risk-free rate (MetricsError::InvalidRiskFreeRate). metrics adds no broker/adapter/live dependency and carries no vendor SDK token, so the family is structurally independent of the IB account; lib.rs re-exports `pub mod metrics;`. The Rust integration test srs_bt_004_metrics drives compute() end to end from a real BacktestResult. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-004 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-004 passes:false because the live dashboard reporting path and the paper/live runtime accumulators that feed this family at runtime (the SRS-SIM-004 snapshot reserves the metrics slot for them, still empty until an accumulator exists), and the SRS-BT-005 benchmark-resolution surface (resolving SPY's actual price history and wiring the dashboard/report benchmark identification), are deferred owners.

baseline.drawdown_peak_token: let mut peak = starting_equity_minor as f64

deferred.what: SYS-16 / SYS-86 require these metrics to be reported for completed backtests, paper strategies, AND the live dashboard. This slice ships the deterministic computation, but the dashboard/API path that surfaces them at a <= 5s refresh (SYS-36) is the SRS-UI / SRS-API owner, so the live-reporting half is deferred and feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-004 passes:false.

Computing the family for a paper or live strategy needs a runtime that accumulates the mark-to-market equity curve and trade log over the strategy's life and feeds this module on demand. The SRS-SIM-004 snapshot reserves a metrics slot for exactly this accumulated state (still empty until an accumulator exists). The accumulator is owned by the paper/live execution runtime, so the paper/live half is deferred.

The backtest path passes BacktestResult's request.starting_cash_minor; the deferred paper/live accumulators will likewise pass their own starting equity. Resolving and threading that starting equity from each runtime mode is the producer's responsibility; this slice fully handles the baseline at the metric boundary and tests it (a single-mark curve still has a first period from the baseline, and an initial drawdown below starting equity is captured).

Resolving SPY's (or a user-selected benchmark's) actual historical price level series from the data layer, and wiring the dashboard/backtest report to identify the benchmark, is SRS-BT-005 (SYS-17, SYS-37). SRS-BT-005 remains passes:false; only its SPY-default selection mechanism is seeded here.

deferred.what: Factor returns, information coefficient, and turnover analysis (the tear-sheet) are SRS-BT-006 (SYS-18) and the atp-factor-pipeline crate, a separate feature. This module is the core SYS-16 metric family only.

sim_benchmark_contract.description: SRS-BT-005 (compare strategy performance against a user-selected benchmark defaulting to SPY; SyRS SYS-17 / SYS-36 / SYS-37; StRS SN-1.04). The benchmark selection, resolution-seam, and comparison-report surface lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `benchmark`) and WRAPS the SRS-BT-004 metric family: the SESSION 44 `metrics` slice already computes alpha/beta given a Benchmark (SPY default) and a BenchmarkPoint level series, so this slice adds the three pieces SRS-BT-005 names. (1) Selection: BenchmarkSelection resolves to SPY when the operator selects none (selection.resolve() applies self.selected.clone().unwrap_or_default(), the single place the SYS-17 SPY default is applied) and validates a user-selected ticker via Benchmark::new, mapping a malformed (empty / non-canonical) symbol to BenchmarkError::UnknownBenchmark so a report can never identify a benchmark it could not honestly name; is_default() lets a report state the comparison used the default. (2) Resolution: BenchmarkSource is the port that turns the resolved Benchmark into the integer-minor level series compute needs (symbol() plus levels(symbol, baseline_ts, axis) returning the pre-trade baseline level then one level per equity-curve mark in order, i.e. axis.len()+1 points). It mirrors the backtest::BarSource deferral seam: the real stored-data resolver is deferred (owner SRS-DATA-007, the unified historical data interface) and a fixture implementation drives the tests. The port is also an OPERATIONAL trust boundary: a real resolver's read can fail in ways that are not a malformed series (timeout, unavailable, not-found, or stale), so levels() surfaces a typed SourceFailure via BenchmarkError::SourceUnavailable, letting a caller retry / alert / fail closed rather than misclassifying an operational failure (stale-data blocking is one such outcome). (3) Comparison report: BenchmarkComparison is the data shape a dashboard/backtest report renders to IDENTIFY and contrast against its benchmark -- benchmark_symbol (the identity), is_default_benchmark, and the alpha, beta, strategy_total_return, benchmark_total_return, and excess_return ratios, each Option<f64> (None when undefined, never a fabricated zero). BenchmarkReport bundles the full PerformanceMetrics family with that comparison. compare() is BOUND TO THE STRATEGY RUN's evaluation window (a DateRange, e.g. BacktestResult.range), not a free baseline timestamp: it rejects an inverted window (InvalidWindow), requires every equity mark to fall within the window (EquityMarkOutsideWindow), and resolves the benchmark baseline AS-OF window.start (baseline_ts = window.start), which must open strictly before the first mark (WindowStartNotBeforeRun). This prevents a stale or unrelated baseline from measuring the benchmark over a different period than the strategy. (The full atomic run-snapshot identity -- a producer-stamped run id binding equity curve, trade log, and benchmark window into one snapshot -- is the deferred accumulator's responsibility, the same boundary as SRS-BT-004; this window binding is the in-scope coherence guard.) compare() then requires the source to serve the SAME symbol the report identifies (BenchmarkError::SourceSymbolMismatch), asks the source for the aligned series, and re-validates that series at the trust boundary BEFORE any metric is reported -- length == axis.len()+1 (SourceLengthMismatch), every level ts aligned to the baseline / mark (SourceTimestampMismatch), and every level strictly positive (NonPositiveSourceLevel) -- and only then calls metrics::compute, wrapping its MetricsError as BenchmarkError::Metrics (defense-in-depth re-validation). The numeric boundary is inherited from the metric family: levels and equity enter in integer minor units (level_minor: i64) and the comparison emits dimensionless f64 ratios (the metric domain, not a money leak); every emitted ratio is verified finite via fn finite_opt + is_finite() and fails closed (BenchmarkError::NonFiniteComparison) so a NaN/inf never reaches a ranking. The work is deterministic (SRS-BT-010): fixed left-to-right folds, no parallelism / RNG / wall-clock read. benchmark adds no broker/adapter dependency and carries no vendor SDK token; lib.rs re-exports `pub mod benchmark;`. The Rust integration test srs_bt_005_benchmark drives compare() end to end against a fixture source over a real BacktestEngine equity curve, including the foreign-window and source-unavailable degraded paths. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-005 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-005 passes:false because resolving the benchmark's ACTUAL historical level series from stored data is the deferred SRS-DATA-007 owner behind BenchmarkSource, and the dashboard/backtest report RENDERING that identifies the benchmark to an operator at the SYS-36 (<=5s) refresh is the deferred SRS-UI / SRS-API owner consuming BenchmarkComparison; only the deterministic selection + resolution-seam + comparison surface is shipped here.

deferred.feature: Benchmark level-series resolution from stored data
deferred.what: compare() resolves a Benchmark and asks a BenchmarkSource for its integer-minor level series, but resolving SPY's (or a user-selected benchmark's) ACTUAL historical price levels from the data layer is SRS-DATA-007 (the unified historical data interface), which is not built. This slice ships the BenchmarkSource port (with its typed SourceFailure operational-failure contract) and a fixture implementation for tests; the real stored-data resolver is the deferred owner, so SRS-BT-005 keeps passes:false.

compare() requires the benchmark baseline (levels[0]) to be strictly before the first equity mark (the pre-trade prior close), and binds the comparison to the run's inclusive window (equity marks within [start, end]). Verifying the baseline is the IMMEDIATE prior close -- not an arbitrarily stale earlier observation -- requires the data layer's actual bar grid (the spacing/calendar), which is the deferred SRS-DATA-007 resolver's responsibility; that resolver, having the bar grid, returns the correct prior close. The in-scope, deterministically-verifiable guard is that the baseline precedes the first mark and the equity curve falls within the window.

BenchmarkComparison is the data shape that identifies the benchmark (symbol, default flag, alpha/beta, total/excess return), but rendering it on the dashboard and in backtest reports at the SYS-36 (<=5s) refresh / SYS-37 is the SRS-UI / SRS-API owner, which is not built. The data contract is shipped here; the rendering surface is deferred.

compare() binds the comparison to the strategy run's evaluation window (DateRange) and validates the equity curve falls within it, but the FULL run identity -- a producer-stamped run id binding the equity curve, trade log, and benchmark window into one consistent snapshot -- needs the deferred paper/live accumulator and an SRS-BT-001 run id, the same boundary deferred by SRS-BT-004. The window binding is the in-scope coherence guard.

deferred.feature: Persisting the benchmark comparison into the queryable backtest record
deferred.what: SRS-BT-009 (persist completed backtest results) names benchmark comparison as a queryable persisted field. Threading BenchmarkComparison into the persisted, queryable backtest record is the SRS-BT-009 owner (the natural next pick), not this slice.

sim_backtest_store_contract.description: SRS-BT-009 (persist completed backtest results; SyRS SYS-21 / SYS-79; StRS SN-1.02 / SN-1.04). The completed-backtest result persistence + query surface lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `backtest_store`) and WRAPS the producer types the prior slices already build -- it recomputes nothing. The acceptance criterion names seven artifacts that must be queryable by strategy, date range, and parameter set: BacktestRecord bundles all seven -- the BacktestRequest parameters (the parameter set), the SRS-BT-004 PerformanceMetrics family, the Vec<Fill> trade log, the Vec<EquityPoint> equity curve, the SRS-BT-005 BenchmarkComparison (the record's benchmark identity), an opaque caller-supplied CodeVersion (the strategy code version), and a producer-supplied completed_at_ts (the timestamp). RunId is the record's identity; BacktestRecord::new and BacktestResultStore::insert share one fail-closed validate_record (rejecting an empty id, an inverted run window, a negative starting cash, an invalid cost family, an equity mark outside the run window, a missing benchmark identity, or a non-finite ratio). BacktestResultStore answers the three SRS-BT-009 query axes -- query_by_strategy, query_by_run_window (the SYS-21 date-range axis -- records whose backtest run window request.range OVERLAPS the queried period, e.g. every 2020 backtest regardless of when it executed), query_by_completion_window (the distinct completion-timestamp axis), query_by_parameter_set (the StrategyParameters tuned set -- the named values that tell two points of a parameter sweep apart, distinct from the launch BacktestRequest), and a combined RecordQuery that ANDs them -- and holds records in a single canonical (completed_at_ts, run_id) order (a total order because run ids are unique, enforced by insert rejecting StoreError::DuplicateRunId), so every query returns deterministically and the serialized form is byte-identical for the same record set regardless of insertion order. serialize/restore is a deterministic, dependency-free text codec (MAGIC header + FNV-1a integrity checksum over the body, length-prefixed strings, sorted records, enum-tagged cost models): restore validates the magic, the checksum BEFORE building any state, the schema version, and every record invariant, decoding into a local Vec and sorting ONCE by order_key (O(n log n) bulk restore, not the O(n^2) of a per-record sorted insert) before constructing the store so a corrupt / truncated / checksum-mismatching blob yields no partially-restored store (StoreError::CorruptRecord, ChecksumMismatch, UnknownSchemaVersion). The numeric boundary: trade-log/equity money stays integer minor units (i128::from over the i64 *_minor fields) end to end, while the metric/comparison ratios are dimensionless f64 (the metric domain, inherited from SRS-BT-004/005, not a money leak) round-tripped EXACTLY via f64::to_bits / f64::from_bits and verified finite on restore (is_finite, StoreError::NonFiniteRatio) so a NaN/inf can never re-enter a ranking from a tampered blob. The work is deterministic (SRS-BT-010): fixed folds, no parallelism / RNG / wall-clock read; the completion timestamp is supplied by the producer. backtest_store adds no broker/adapter dependency and carries no vendor SDK token; lib.rs re-exports `pub mod backtest_store;`. The Rust integration test srs_bt_009_backtest_store drives a real BacktestEngine run -> metrics::compute -> benchmark::compare -> BacktestRecord -> store insert -> serialize -> restore -> query by all three axes end to end, including the corrupt-blob fail-closed and duplicate-run-id degraded paths. The FNV-1a integrity checksum is non-cryptographic: it detects ACCIDENTAL corruption (a change that does not also recompute it), NOT a deliberate checksum-recomputing tamperer (that needs a keyed MAC + key management, out of scope for the single-user, local-only baseline, the same stance as paper_state); the per-record invariant + domain checks are the independent defense that still rejects an impossible-valued recomputed blob. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-009 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-009 passes:false because writing the serialized blob to the SSD-primary / NAS-archival tier is the deferred SRS-DATA-008 owner, rendering the queryable history to an operator in the dashboard / backtest report is the deferred SRS-UI-004 / SRS-API owner, and a full orchestrated run stamping a real run id / code version / completion timestamp end to end (the atomic run-snapshot identity) is the deferred SRS-BT-001 / orchestrator owner -- only the deterministic record + store + query + codec surface is shipped here.

deferred.what: serialize/restore round-trips the whole store through a deterministic, dependency-free text blob (the in-scope 'persist' verb at the data-shape level), but writing that blob to the SSD-primary / NAS-archival tier (and reading it back tiered) is SRS-DATA-008, which is not built. So SRS-BT-009 keeps passes:false.

BacktestResultStore answers the three SRS-BT-009 query axes in-process, but rendering the queryable history TO AN OPERATOR (the backtest result history view, SYS-21) is the SRS-UI-004 / SRS-API owner consuming BacktestRecord, which is not built. The data + query contract is shipped here; the rendering surface is deferred.

BacktestRecord accepts a caller-supplied RunId, CodeVersion, and completed_at_ts and persists them, but a full orchestrated run that STAMPS a real run id, the strategy container image digest as the code version, and the completion timestamp end to end is SRS-BT-001 / the orchestrator. This slice persists provenance; it does not produce it.

A record binds its parameters, metrics, trade log, equity curve, and benchmark comparison together and validates the equity curve falls within the run window (the in-scope coherence guard), but the FULL run identity -- a producer-stamped run id atomically binding the equity curve, trade log, and benchmark window into one consistent snapshot -- needs the deferred paper/live accumulator + an SRS-BT-001 run id, the same boundary deferred by SRS-BT-004/005. Likewise, full VALUE-LEVEL metric verification (recomputing all eight metrics + alpha/beta from the stored curve/trade-log and confirming they match the stored values) needs the MetricsConfig (periods-per-year, risk-free rate) and the benchmark level series, neither of which SRS-BT-009 lists among its persisted fields; metric CORRECTNESS is the SRS-BT-004 producer's responsibility, and this layer enforces STRUCTURAL coherence (curve shape, trade-log ordering/window, benchmark identity, finiteness) -- the value-binding is this same deferred boundary.

factor_analysis_contract.description: SRS-BT-006 (produce factor analysis and tear-sheet outputs; SyRS SYS-18; StRS SN-1.05). The deterministic, dependency-free factor-analysis surface lives in crates/atp-factor-pipeline (module `factor_analysis`). compute_tear_sheet consumes a FactorPanel -- a per-rebalance-period panel of (SecurityKey, factor value, forward return) observations plus a quantile count -- and returns one FactorTearSheet bundling the three deliverables SYS-18 names: (1) InformationCoefficient, the per-period Spearman rank correlation between factor value and forward return (average tie ranks; Pearson of ranks; clamped to its [-1,1] domain) with mean / std / risk-adjusted IC; (2) FactorReturns, the per-period quantile-sorted mean returns plus the top-minus-bottom long-short spread with mean and compounded cumulative spread; (3) TurnoverAnalysis, the per-period top/bottom-quantile membership churn with means. Factor values and returns are dimensionless f64 (the factor domain, not a money leak); the work is deterministic (fixed left-to-right folds; cross-sections sorted by the total order (factor_value, SecurityKey)); an undefined statistic is None (never a fabricated zero); a non-finite aggregate fails closed; and FactorPanel::validate rejects an empty or degenerate panel at the trust boundary. Deferred (SRS-BT-006 stays passes:false): the scheduled full-universe factor job that produces the panel (SRS-FAC-001), wiring real factor values/returns from the unified historical data interface (SRS-DATA-007), rendering the tear-sheet to an operator (SRS-UI / SRS-API), and bundling the SRS-BT-004 PerformanceMetrics family into one cross-crate report.

separation.comment: The spread/turnover are factor-attributable only when the factor strictly separates the top and bottom quantiles AT BOTH BOUNDING CUTOFFS (q0|q1 and q(Q-2)|q(Q-1)), not just extreme-to-extreme -- for 3+ quantiles a tie can straddle an inner cutoff and decide the bottom/top composition by SecurityKey. Otherwise the value is withheld (None), so a constant or cutoff-tied factor cannot fabricate alpha.

deferred: the scheduled full-universe factor job that produces the panel (SRS-FAC-001)
wiring real factor values and forward returns from the unified historical data interface (SRS-DATA-007)
rendering the factor tear-sheet to an operator (SRS-UI / SRS-API)
bundling the SRS-BT-004 PerformanceMetrics family into one cross-crate tear-sheet report
